call_meta.employee_count_estimate: mid-market, 15 sales reps across US and Europe
next_steps: Send SOC 2 compliance status and trust page
Share customer case studies for hardware/physical goods companies
Prospect presents findings to stakeholders within two weeks (by May 20)
If shortlisted, schedule reverse demo with sales engineer and provide approval workflows, price books, and dummy line items
call_meta.budget_indication: Enterprise license discussed at $20K-$25K/year range; no budget objection raised

jtbd.job: Automate the quote approval process to enforce discount governance and technical review before quotes go out
jtbd.context: Current approval process is manual via email and Slack. No enforcement mechanism to prevent reps from sending highly discounted quotes without approval. Process is not auditable.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Conditional multi-level approvals triggered by discount thresholds and regional routing, plus mandatory technical review by application scientists on every outgoing quote

jtbd.job: Build a scalable quoting infrastructure that supports organizational growth
jtbd.context: Current manual process works with 15 reps but the prospect explicitly wants to prepare for future headcount growth. Volume is low today (5-10 quotes/week) but the organization expects to scale.
jtbd.desired_outcome: A quoting system with automated rules, price books, and calculated pricing that does not require manual intervention as the team grows

jtbd.job: Centralize pricing governance with price books for different customer segments and regions
jtbd.context: Currently limited to US and Europe price books managed outside HubSpot. Foresees needing segment-specific price books for universities, government, industry, and SMEs.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Price books managed inside HubSpot that automatically apply the correct pricing based on customer type and region

pains.pain: Manual approval process via email and Slack with no enforcement or audit trail
pains.trigger: Every quote requiring discount approval or technical review. Reps must manually circulate quotes through email and Slack to collect approvals from multiple stakeholders.
pains.cost: Unenforceable discount governance: reps can send highly discounted quotes without approval. No auditability on when or whether quotes were approved. Additional admin burden on every sales rep for every quote.
pains.urgency_reason: Primary driver of the evaluation. Prospect opened the call with this pain and it shaped the entire conversation.

pains.pain: HubSpot Commerce Hub is immature and failed to meet CPQ requirements
pains.trigger: Attempted migration from legacy quotes to Commerce Hub. Found the product lacking in approval workflows, calculated pricing, and price book support.
pains.cost: Wasted evaluation time on Commerce Hub. Still stuck on legacy quotes with no path forward inside HubSpot's native tooling.
pains.urgency_reason: Already tried and rejected HubSpot's own solution, actively evaluating third-party alternatives

pains.pain: No calculated pricing or product rules in current quoting setup
pains.trigger: Need to calculate service package pricing as a percentage of physical product value (e.g., implementation fee = 10% of hardware). Currently done manually by reps.
pains.cost: Manual calculation errors, inconsistent pricing across reps, no way to enforce pricing formulas automatically.
pains.urgency_reason: Product library is small (15-20 SKUs) and configuration is simple today, but this will compound as the catalog grows

pains.pain: No price book functionality in HubSpot legacy quotes
pains.trigger: Selling across US and Europe with different regional pricing. Foresees needing segment-specific price books (university, government, industry) that legacy quotes cannot support.
pains.cost: Reps reference external spreadsheets for pricing, introducing friction and potential for pricing errors. No automation for customer-type-specific pricing.
pains.urgency_reason: Currently manageable with two regional price books, but anticipated to become acute as customer segmentation grows

pains.pain: Legacy quote templates lack flexibility for professional output
pains.trigger: Need for customizable templates with payment terms, additional properties, and line item formatting that legacy templates cannot provide.
pains.cost: Templates are rigid. Cannot add custom fields like payment terms at the point of quote creation.
pains.urgency_reason: Acknowledged as a limitation but not a primary evaluation driver

pains.pain: SOC 2 compliance is a hard requirement and Quotivity's certification is pending
pains.trigger: IT security team requires SOC 2 compliance for all software vendors. Prospect noticed the trust page listed end-of-April for SOC 2, but it is now May.
pains.cost: Potential blocker to deal progression. Cannot move forward with procurement without SOC 2 certification.
pains.urgency_reason: Hard requirement from IT. Prospect explicitly called this out twice and flagged the missed April deadline.

workflow_reality.current_process: Reps create quotes using HubSpot legacy quotes. Approvals are collected manually via email and Slack with no structured workflow. Discount thresholds (20%, 30%, 40%) require escalation to progressively senior approvers. Every quote also requires a technical review by an application scientist. Price books are managed externally (likely spreadsheets) with US and Europe regional splits. Products are already in the HubSpot product library (15-20 SKUs).
tools_in_use: HubSpot Sales Hub (CRM and legacy quotes)

team_size_and_structure.revops: Sales operations manager (prospect) managing HubSpot administration

workflow_reality.quote_volume: 5-10 per week (low volume, high value physical goods)
brittleness_indicators: Approval process has no enforcement: reps can bypass it entirely
No audit trail for approvals (scattered across email and Slack)
Regional routing is manual and relies on reps knowing who to ask
Price books live outside HubSpot, creating a split workflow
Prospect explicitly said current process works only because the team is still small

evaluated.name: HubSpot Commerce Hub
evaluated.why_they_passed: Not a mature product. Lacked the CPQ functionality needed for approvals, calculated pricing, and price books.
evaluated.key_quote: We tried the Commerce Hub, HubSpot's new CPQ package. But it's not really a mature product.

current_workaround.description: HubSpot legacy quotes for quote generation, email and Slack for manual approval routing, external spreadsheets for price book reference
current_workaround.adequacy: Functional at current scale (15 reps, 5-10 quotes/week) but prospect explicitly flags it as unscalable. No enforcement, no auditability, and growing segment-specific pricing needs will break it.

quotes.quote: Our quotes approval process is very manual at the moment. It's done via email, via Slack, so it's very manual process.
quotes.usage_notes: Clear articulation of the core pain. Opens with the problem statement. Strong for content targeting RevOps leaders stuck on manual approvals.

quotes.quote: We can't really enforce people to get approval before they're sending out a highly discounted quote.
quotes.usage_notes: Captures the enforcement gap that drives discount leakage. Strong for content about CPQ policy enforcement and margin protection. Resonates with Finance/Controller persona.

quotes.quote: It's not auditable, the current manual process, because the information is logged either in email or in Slack, so it's not really easy to track.
quotes.usage_notes: Connects approval pain to compliance and governance. Useful for content targeting regulated industries or companies with audit requirements.

quotes.quote: We tried the Commerce Hub, HubSpot's new CPQ package. But it's not really a mature product.
quotes.usage_notes: Direct competitive intelligence against HubSpot Commerce Hub. Perfect for comparison content and positioning Quotivity as the upgrade path.

quotes.quote: Currently still manageable, but we want to think of a more scalable solution in case the organization grows in the future.
quotes.usage_notes: Captures the forward-looking buyer who is building for scale, not just fixing today's problem. Good for content about building quoting infrastructure before it breaks.

quotes.quote: The legacy quote template is not very flexible.
quotes.usage_notes: Reinforces the limitations of HubSpot native quoting. Supports RevOps-focused content about template management pain.

quotes.quote: SOC 2 compliant, that's basically the hard requirements from our IT side. Because our industry, it's quite important to have every software SOC 2 compliant.
quotes.usage_notes: Important deal intelligence. Reveals that security compliance is a gating factor for hardware/scientific instrument companies evaluating SaaS tools.

quotes.quote: Because it's manual, the sales rep always need to generate the quotes, they have to send around the quotes to get different type of approvals. So it's also additional admin work for the sales rep.
quotes.usage_notes: Connects manual approvals to rep productivity loss. Bridges RevOps pain to VP Sales concern about rep time spent on admin instead of selling.

deviant_terms.meaning: The manual process of circulating a quote document to multiple stakeholders for approval via email and Slack
deviant_terms.frequency: used once, but captures the exact workflow friction

deviant_terms.meaning: Routing approvals and applying price books based on whether the deal is US or Europe
deviant_terms.frequency: used once, clearly an established internal concept

deviant_terms.meaning: Technical review by application scientists to verify quote configuration accuracy before sending
deviant_terms.frequency: used once, describes the non-discount approval layer

deviant_terms.term: hard requirements from our IT side
deviant_terms.meaning: Non-negotiable security compliance prerequisites (SOC 2) that gate vendor selection
deviant_terms.frequency: used twice, clearly a firm organizational policy

keyword_candidates.source: primary pain — prospect's entire evaluation is driven by the lack of automated approval workflows in HubSpot
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How to build multi-level quote approval workflows in HubSpot (and why native tools fall short)

keyword_candidates.source: direct pain — manual approval via email and Slack because HubSpot has no native approval engine for quotes
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why HubSpot's native CPQ can't enforce quote approvals (and what mid-market teams do instead)

keyword_candidates.keyword: HubSpot Commerce Hub vs legacy quotes
keyword_candidates.source: prospect explicitly tried Commerce Hub and rejected it as immature, then fell back to legacy quotes
keyword_candidates.content_angle: HubSpot Commerce Hub vs. legacy quotes: what changed, what didn't, and when you need a third option

keyword_candidates.source: prospect cannot enforce discount thresholds — reps can send highly discounted quotes without approval
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How mid-market companies stop discount leakage without slowing down their sales team

keyword_candidates.source: prospect needs regional and segment-specific price books that HubSpot legacy quotes do not support
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How to manage multiple price books in HubSpot for regional and segment-based pricing

keyword_candidates.source: prospect sells physical/hardware goods with configuration needs — maps to manufacturing ICP segment
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why manufacturers on HubSpot outgrow native quoting (and what to do about it)

keyword_candidates.source: prospect is actively evaluating DealHub alongside Quotivity
keyword_candidates.content_angle: DealHub vs. HubSpot-native CPQ: when enterprise complexity isn't worth the implementation cost

keyword_candidates.source: prospect needs service pricing calculated as a percentage of physical product value on the quote
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How to set up calculated pricing rules in your CPQ (percentage-based, tiered, and formula-driven)
